one_line_takeaway: Token-level LLM watermarks (KGW, EXP) that achieve >88% detection accuracy on benign Bangla text collapse to 9-19% detection accuracy under a simple Bangla-English-Bangla round-trip translation attack, and layering a post-generation Waterfall watermark on top only partially restores robustness (to 40-50%) at the cost of added semantic drift.
key_finding: Under benign (non-attacked) conditions, single-layer watermarking transfers well to Bangla: KGW achieves 0.885 detection accuracy and EXP 0.912 (Table 2), with minimal fluency loss (avg. watermarked perplexity 2.309 for KGW vs. 2.1616 unwatermarked; 2.0295 for EXP vs. 2.1368 unwatermarked) and high lexical similarity (ROUGE-1 0.3671/0.3854, ROUGE-2 0.3039/0.3207, ROUGE-L 0.3601/0.3784 for KGW/EXP respectively). After the RTT attack, both methods collapse: Table 2 reports overall 'Robustness to RTT' of 0.09 (KGW) and 0.13 (EXP); the per-length breakdown in Figure 3 shows detection accuracy falling from 87-91% pre-RTT to 15-19% post-RTT across the 100/150/200-token lengths, i.e. below 20% in every configuration, with EXP retaining a slightly heavier tail of high-confidence scores post-RTT (Figure 5) than KGW (Figure 4). The proposed layered (embedding-time + Waterfall) watermarking raises post-RTT detection accuracy to 40-50%, a 25-35 percentage-point absolute improvement and a stated 3x-4x relative improvement over single-layer post-RTT accuracy, at the cost of moderate semantic-similarity degradation shown in Figure 7 (semantic scores roughly in the 0.70-0.90 range for KGW/EXP under double watermarking, read from the plotted axis).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is: (1) the first systematic robustness evaluation of two embedding-time LLM watermarking methods, KGW (Kirchenbauer et al. 2023) and Exponential Sampling/EXP (Kuditipudi et al. 2023), specifically for Bangla text generation under a Bangla->English->Bangla round-trip translation (RTT) attack; and (2) a proposed layered/double-layer watermarking scheme that stacks a post-generation Waterfall watermark (Lau et al. 2024, an existing prior method reused, not invented here) on top of the embedding-time watermark, and an empirical demonstration that this layering meaningfully improves post-RTT detection accuracy at the cost of moderate semantic drift. KGW, EXP, and Waterfall are all pre-existing algorithms from prior work; the paper's novelty is the Bangla-language evaluation, the RTT threat analysis, and the composition/ablation of these existing techniques, not a new watermarking algorithm.
threat_model: Black-box adversary with no access to model internals, token-level probabilities, or watermarking keys during generation, attack, or detection. The adversary's goal is to remove/weaken an embedded watermark signal from Bangla LLM-generated text while preserving semantic meaning, using a single realistic laundering attack: round-trip translation (Bangla -> English -> Bangla) via the BanglaNMT (csebuetnlp/banglat5_nmt_en_bn) model. The paper explicitly does not model adaptive attackers who choose secondary watermark layers based on inferred deployment context (left to future work), and restricts itself to one RTT pathway rather than multi-hop translation or stronger paraphrasers.
limitations: Limitations stated explicitly by the authors (Section 8): (1) evaluation covers only a single RTT pathway (Bangla->English->Bangla), not multi-hop or alternative cross-lingual paraphrasing attacks; (2) the layered-watermarking experiments are restricted to 100-token generations due to computational constraints, so how layered robustness scales with longer text is untested; (3) ROUGE and perplexity are used as fluency/semantic proxies but may not capture subtle meaning drift in morphologically rich Bangla, and no human evaluation was performed; (4) only a fixed set of algorithms (KGW, EXP, Waterfall) was tested, with no adaptive or learned layer-selection strategies, and no adaptive-attacker threat model was considered. Additional grounding-note caveats: the paper studies only one base model (Bangla LLaMA-3-8B) and one RTT translation system (BanglaNMT), so generalization across models/translators is unverified; code/data were stated as 'to be made public' but not yet released at submission.

datasets_used: Bangla-Alpaca Orca dataset (filtered subset, 500 evaluation prompts)
quantitative_results: KGW avg. watermarked perplexity 2.309 vs unwatermarked 2.1616 (Table 2)
EXP avg. watermarked perplexity 2.0295 vs unwatermarked 2.1368 (Table 2)
Overall robustness to RTT (Table 2 summary metric): KGW 0.09, EXP 0.13
Per-length pre-RTT detection accuracy (Figure 3): KGW 87-89% and EXP 89-91% across 100/150/200 tokens
Per-length post-RTT detection accuracy (Figure 3): KGW 15-17% and EXP 18-19% across 100/150/200 tokens
Layered watermarking post-RTT detection accuracy: 40-50%, a 25-35 percentage-point absolute gain and stated 3x-4x relative improvement over single-layer post-RTT accuracy (100-token generations only)

Qualitative single-example detection scores (Table 3): KGW watermarked output score 2.20x10^-5 (detected) vs unwatermarked 0.529 (not detected); EXP watermarked output score 1.89x10^-6 (detected) vs unwatermarked 0.814 (not detected)
baselines_compared: KGW soft-biasing watermark (Kirchenbauer et al., 2023) - embedding-time baseline
Exponential Sampling / EXP watermark (Kuditipudi et al., 2023) - embedding-time baseline
Waterfall post-generation watermark (Lau et al., 2024) - used as the post-generation layer, also evaluated alone in the ablation
Unwatermarked generation - baseline for perplexity/ROUGE/detection-score comparisons
